message:
results: SWE-bench evaluation — 3 real-world tasks × 4 strategies

Tasks: django-16560, django-11885, pytest-5787
Model: gpt-5.2, threshold-scale 0.15

Key findings:
- recall: best efficiency + retention on complex tasks
- session-log: runaway risk (200 turns on pytest-5787)
- ooda: strong retention on complex tasks (matched recall)
- compact: baseline, lowest retention under heavy compaction

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -0,0 +1,30 @@
+{
+  "strategy": "compact",
+  "model": "gpt-5.2",
+  "task": "Fix the following Django issue:\n\nCombine fast delete queries\n\nWhen emulating ON DELETE CASCADE via on_delete=models.CASCADE, the deletion.Collector will try to perform fast queries which are DELETE FROM table WHERE table.pk IN .... There are a few conditions required for this fast path to be taken, but when this happens the collection logic should combine such queries by table to reduce the number of roundtrips to the database.\n\nFor example, given the following models:\n```python\nclass Person(models.Model):\n    friends = models.ManyToManyField('self')\n\nclass User(models.Model):\n    pass\n\nclass Entry(models.Model):\n    created_by = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)\n    updated_by = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)\n```\n\nDeleting a User instance will generate 2 separate DELETE queries against the Entry table (one for created_by, one for updated_by) when they could be combined into a single query using OR.\n\nYour task:\n1. Read the deletion module: django/db/models/deletion.py\n2. Read the related SQL compiler: django/db/models/sql/subqueries.py\n3. Read the test file: tests/delete/tests.py\n4. Understand how the Collector.fast_deletes list works and how delete() processes it\n5. Modify the deletion logic to combine fast_deletes targeting the same table\n6. The failing test to make pass: test_fast_delete_combined_relationships (delete.tests.FastDeleteTests)\n7. Run tests: python -m pytest tests/delete/tests.py -x -v\n8. Make sure no existing tests break",
+  "completed": true,
+  "turn_count": 25,
+  "total_input_tokens": 276218,
+  "total_output_tokens": 1694,
+  "total_tokens": 277912,
+  "recall_calls": 0,
+  "fork_summary_calls": 0,
+  "compaction_events": 12,
+  "compaction_layers": [
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask",
+    "observation_mask"
+  ],
+  "retention_score": 0.65,
+  "duration_seconds": 45.628351584,
+  "result": "{\"decision\":\"implemented\",\"message\":\"Combined Collector.fast_deletes by target table using queryset OR before executing _raw_delete(), reducing duplicate DELETEs for multiple CASCADE relationships to the same model/table.\",\"data\":{},\"artifacts\":[\"django/db/models/deletion.py\"]}"
+}

@@ -0,0 +1,39 @@
+# SWE-bench Evaluation: django__django-11885
+
+**Task:** Combine fast delete queries in Django's deletion.Collector
+**Model:** gpt-5.2 (OpenAI)
+**Threshold scale:** 0.15
+**Date:** 2026-02-13
+
+## Results
+
+| Strategy    | Turns | Total Tokens | Compaction Events | Retention Score | Task Completed | Duration (s) |
+|-------------|------:|-------------:|------------------:|----------------:|:--------------:|-------------:|
+| compact     |    25 |      277,912 |                12 |            0.65 |      Yes       |        45.6  |
+| recall      |    92 |    1,710,387 |               117 |            0.70 |      Yes       |       204.2  |
+| session-log |    38 |      496,768 |                32 |            0.60 |      Yes       |       135.4  |
+| ooda        |    95 |    1,532,593 |               119 |            0.65 |      Yes       |       342.0  |
+
+## Observations
+
+- **All 4 strategies completed the task successfully.** Each agent implemented the fast-delete combining logic and reported passing tests.
+- **compact** was the most efficient by a wide margin: fewest turns (25), fewest tokens (278k), fewest compactions (12), and fastest wall time (46s). It achieved a retention score of 0.65.
+- **recall** used the most total tokens (1.71M) with 92 turns and 117 compaction events, but achieved the highest retention score (0.70). Its result message suggests it reverted test changes rather than implementing the fix from scratch, raising a question about whether its solution approach differed.
+- **session-log** was a middle ground: 38 turns, 497k tokens, 32 compaction events, 135s. It had the lowest retention score (0.60) despite the session-log mechanism.
+- **ooda** was the slowest (342s) with the most turns (95) and second-highest token usage (1.53M). It used 119 compaction events and 7 session-log checkpoints. Retention score matched compact at 0.65.
+
+## Token Efficiency (tokens per turn)
+
+| Strategy    | Tokens/Turn |
+|-------------|------------:|
+| compact     |      11,116 |
+| recall      |      18,591 |
+| session-log |      13,073 |
+| ooda        |      16,132 |
+
+## Key Takeaways
+
+1. **compact dominated on efficiency** -- it solved the task in under a minute with 5-6x fewer tokens than the recall and ooda strategies.
+2. **recall had the best retention** (0.70) but at enormous cost -- 6.2x the tokens of compact.
+3. **ooda was the most expensive overall** in wall time, despite not achieving better retention or fewer turns than recall.
+4. The low threshold-scale (0.15) triggered aggressive compaction across all strategies, which is reflected in the high compaction event counts for recall (117) and ooda (119).

@@ -0,0 +1,114 @@
+{
+  "strategy": "compact",
+  "model": "gpt-5.2",
+  "task": "Fix the following Django issue:\n\nAllow to customize the code attribute of ValidationError raised by BaseConstraint.validate\n\nIt is currently possible to customize the violation_error_message of a ValidationError raised by a constraint but not the code. We need to add a new violation_error_code parameter to BaseConstraint to allow customizing the error code.\n\nCurrently, to achieve the same result, you have to subclass the constraint to tweak validate to catch and reraise the ValidationError. Since the documentation recommends providing a descriptive error code to the constructor when raising a ValidationError, it makes sense to provide this possibility for errors raised by constraints.\n\nThe hint from a Django maintainer says: \"Agreed, adding violation_error_code sounds like a good idea as we have a similar mechanism for validators.\"\n\nYour task:\n1. Read the relevant source files to understand the current constraint implementation (django/db/models/constraints.py, django/contrib/postgres/constraints.py)\n2. Add a violation_error_code parameter to BaseConstraint.__init__\n3. Pass the violation_error_code to ValidationError in all validate() methods\n4. Update __eq__, __repr__, deconstruct, and clone methods for all constraint classes\n5. Update CheckConstraint, UniqueConstraint, and ExclusionConstraint (postgres) similarly\n6. Run the relevant tests to verify: python -m pytest tests/constraints/tests.py -x -v\n7. Fix any test failures\n\nThe failing tests you need to make pass:\n- test_custom_violation_code_message (constraints.tests.BaseConstraintTests)\n- test_deconstruction (constraints.tests.BaseConstraintTests)\n- test_eq (constraints.tests.CheckConstraintTests)\n- test_repr_with_violation_error_code (constraints.tests.CheckConstraintTests)\n- test_validate_custom_error (constraints.tests.CheckConstraintTests)\n- test_eq (constraints.tests.UniqueConstraintTests)\n- test_repr_with_violation_error_code (constraints.tests.UniqueConstraintTests)\n- test_validate_conditon_custom_error (constraints.tests.UniqueConstraintTests)",

@@ -0,0 +1,35 @@
+# SWE-bench Evaluation: django__django-16560
+
+**Task:** Allow customization of `violation_error_code` on `BaseConstraint.validate`
+**Model:** gpt-5.2 (OpenAI)
+**Threshold scale:** 0.15
+**Date:** 2026-02-13
+
+## Results
+
+| Strategy    | Turns | Total Tokens | Compaction Events | Retention Score | Task Completed | Duration (s) |
+|-------------|------:|-------------:|------------------:|----------------:|:--------------:|-------------:|
+| compact     |    88 |    1,304,625 |                96 |            0.60 |       Yes      |       205.3  |
+| recall      |    55 |      751,211 |                51 |            0.85 |       Yes      |       227.5  |
+| session-log |    69 |    1,007,027 |                70 |            0.80 |       Yes      |       288.5  |
+| ooda        |    90 |    1,427,451 |               110 |            0.85 |       Yes      |       369.4  |
+
+## Observations
+
+- All 4 strategies completed the task successfully.
+- **recall** was the most token-efficient (751K tokens, 55 turns) and achieved the highest retention score (0.85) tied with ooda.
+- **compact** used 1.3M tokens across 88 turns but had the lowest retention score (0.60), suggesting aggressive compaction discarded probe-relevant context.
+- **session-log** landed in the middle on tokens (1.0M) and retention (0.80), with 69 fork-summary calls generating session log entries.
+- **ooda** was the most expensive (1.4M tokens, 90 turns, 369s) and had the most compaction events (110) including 4 session-log checkpoints. Despite high cost, it matched recall's retention score (0.85).
+- Duration varied significantly: compact was fastest (205s), ooda slowest (369s). The extra overhead in ooda comes from orient-phase steering and more frequent compaction.
+
+## Token Efficiency vs Retention
+
+| Strategy    | Tokens per Retention Point | Tokens per Turn |
+|-------------|---------------------------:|----------------:|
+| compact     |                  2,174,375 |          14,825 |
+| recall      |                    883,778 |          13,658 |
+| session-log |                  1,258,784 |          14,594 |
+| ooda        |                  1,679,354 |          15,861 |
+
+**Best overall: recall** -- fewest tokens, fewest turns, highest retention, reasonable duration.

+{

+  "task": "Fix the following pytest issue:\n\nException serialization should include chained exceptions\n\nWhen running tests with chained exceptions (using \"from\" or implicit chaining), pytest displays the whole exception chain nicely when run normally. But when run with xdist (-n auto), it only displays the last exception because the serialization/deserialization of report objects doesn't handle chained exceptions.\n\nThe issue provides these example tests:\n```python\ndef test_chained_exception_with_from():\n    try:\n        try:\n            raise ValueError(11)\n        except Exception as e1:\n            raise ValueError(12) from e1\n    except Exception as e2:\n        raise ValueError(13) from e2\n\ndef test_chained_exception_without_from():\n    try:\n        try:\n            raise ValueError(21)\n        except Exception:\n            raise ValueError(22)\n    except Exception:\n        raise ValueError(23)\n```\n\nWhen run without xdist, all chained exceptions are displayed. When run with xdist, only the outermost exception (ValueError: 13 or ValueError: 23) is shown because the report serialization loses the chain information.\n\nA maintainer noted: \"currently exception serialization is best described as limited and simplistic, that's the main issue there\"\n\nYour task:\n1. Read the report serialization code: src/_pytest/reports.py (focus on _to_json and _from_json methods)\n2. Read the exception representation code: src/_pytest/_code/code.py (focus on ExceptionChainRepr)\n3. Read the test file: testing/test_reports.py\n4. Modify _to_json to serialize chained exceptions (ExceptionChainRepr.chain)\n5. Modify _from_json to deserialize the chain back into ExceptionChainRepr objects\n6. The failing tests to make pass:\n   - testing/test_reports.py::TestReportSerialization::test_chained_exceptions[TestReport]\n   - testing/test_reports.py::TestReportSerialization::test_chained_exceptions[CollectReport]\n7. Run tests: python -m pytest testing/test_reports.py -x -v\n8. Make sure no existing tests break",

+{

+  "task": "Fix the following pytest issue:\n\nException serialization should include chained exceptions\n\nWhen running tests with chained exceptions (using \"from\" or implicit chaining), pytest displays the whole exception chain nicely when run normally. But when run with xdist (-n auto), it only displays the last exception because the serialization/deserialization of report objects doesn't handle chained exceptions.\n\nThe issue provides these example tests:\n```python\ndef test_chained_exception_with_from():\n    try:\n        try:\n            raise ValueError(11)\n        except Exception as e1:\n            raise ValueError(12) from e1\n    except Exception as e2:\n        raise ValueError(13) from e2\n\ndef test_chained_exception_without_from():\n    try:\n        try:\n            raise ValueError(21)\n        except Exception:\n            raise ValueError(22)\n    except Exception:\n        raise ValueError(23)\n```\n\nWhen run without xdist, all chained exceptions are displayed. When run with xdist, only the outermost exception (ValueError: 13 or ValueError: 23) is shown because the report serialization loses the chain information.\n\nA maintainer noted: \"currently exception serialization is best described as limited and simplistic, that's the main issue there\"\n\nYour task:\n1. Read the report serialization code: src/_pytest/reports.py (focus on _to_json and _from_json methods)\n2. Read the exception representation code: src/_pytest/_code/code.py (focus on ExceptionChainRepr)\n3. Read the test file: testing/test_reports.py\n4. Modify _to_json to serialize chained exceptions (ExceptionChainRepr.chain)\n5. Modify _from_json to deserialize the chain back into ExceptionChainRepr objects\n6. The failing tests to make pass:\n   - testing/test_reports.py::TestReportSerialization::test_chained_exceptions[TestReport]\n   - testing/test_reports.py::TestReportSerialization::test_chained_exceptions[CollectReport]\n7. @@ -0,0 +1,30 @@
+# SWE-Bench Evaluation: pytest-dev/pytest-5787
+
+**Task:** Exception serialization should include chained exceptions
+**Model:** gpt-5.2 / openai
+**Threshold scale:** 0.15
+**Date:** 2026-02-13
+
+## Results
+
+| Strategy    | Turns | Total Tokens | Compaction Events | Retention Score | Task Completed | Duration (s) |
+|-------------|------:|-------------:|------------------:|----------------:|:--------------:|-------------:|
+| compact     |    93 |    1,287,323 |                82 |            0.50 |       yes      |        202.9 |
+| recall      |    45 |      608,130 |                37 |            0.55 |       yes      |        110.0 |
+| session-log |   200 |    3,405,222 |               288 |            0.75 |       no       |        770.9 |
+| ooda        |    83 |    1,251,843 |                94 |            0.60 |       yes      |        326.5 |
+
+## Notes
+
+- **compact**: Completed in 93 turns. Reported fixing chained exception repr round-tripping through report serialization and adding a regression test. Lowest retention score (0.50).
+- **recall**: Most efficient run -- fewest turns (45), lowest token usage (608k), fastest wall time (110s). Completed with retention score of 0.55. No recall sub-agent calls were triggered (recall_calls=0).
+- **session-log**: Hit the 200-turn limit and returned an empty result string, indicating it did not produce a final decision. Consumed by far the most tokens (3.4M) and had 198 fork_summary_calls and 288 compaction events. Despite not completing the task, it achieved the highest retention score (0.75), suggesting the session log preserves context well but the overhead may cause the agent to spin.
+- **ooda**: Completed in 83 turns with 83 fork_summary_calls. Reported the task as "resolved" but noted that no code changes were required (earlier failures were due to running against installed pytest instead of checkout). Retention score 0.60.
+
+## Observations
+
+1. **Recall is the clear efficiency winner** on this task: 2x fewer turns than the next best, 2x fewer tokens, fastest wall time.
+2. **Session-log has the highest retention but worst task completion** -- it burned 3.4M tokens across 200 turns without finishing. The overhead of fork-summarize on every turn (198 calls) appears to slow convergence rather than help it.
+3. **Compact and ooda both completed** with similar token budgets (~1.25M), but ooda took 60% longer in wall time due to fork-summarize overhead.
+4. **No strategy triggered recall sub-agent calls** (recall_calls=0 across all 4 runs), which means the recall strategy on this task behaved essentially like compact without the recall overhead.
+5. **Retention scores increase with context strategy complexity** (compact 0.50 < recall 0.55 < ooda 0.60 < session-log 0.75), but task completion does not follow the same trend.
